cot_prefix: Okay, let me try to work through this problem step by step. So, the question is about Jane's birthday and figuring out a specific date one year ago from today. Let me start by understanding all the given information. First, Jane was born on the last day of February in 2001. Now, the last day of February depends on whether it's a leap year or not. Leap years occur every 4 years, right? So, February has 29 days in a leap year and 28 otherwise. Let me check if 2001 is a leap year. To determine if a year is a leap year, the rule is: if the year is divisible by 4, it's a leap year, unless it's a century year (divisible by 100) unless it's also divisible by 400. So 2001 divided by 4 is 500.25, which means it's not divisible by 4. Therefore, 2001 is not a leap year, so the last day of February 2001 would be February 28, 2001. So Jane was born on 02/28/2001. Now, today is her 16th birthday. That means today's date is her 16th birthday. Since she was born in 2001, adding 16 years would take us to 2017. Wait, 2001 + 16 = 2017. So today's date is the same day she was born, but in 2017. So today is 02/28/2017, right? Because she was born on the last day of February, which in 2001 was 02/28, and since 2017 is not a leap year (2017 divided by 4 is 504.25, so not a leap year), the last day of February 2017 is also 02/28. So today is 02/28/2017. The question asks for the date one year ago from today. So subtracting one year from today's date (02/28/2017) would be 02/28/2016. But wait, 2016 is a leap year, right? Because 2016 divided by 4 is 504, which is a whole number, and since it's not a century year, it is a leap year. So February 2016 had 29 days. But when we subtract one year from 02/28/2017, we need to check if there's any issue with leap years. Normally, subtracting a year would just subtract 365 days, but when dealing with dates around leap days, sometimes there can be nuances. However, since we're subtracting exactly one year, the date would be the same day and month but 2016. So 02/28/2017 minus one year is 02/28/2016. Even though 2016 had a February 29th, since the original date is February 28th, that date exists in both leap and non-leap years. Therefore, the date one year ago from today (02/28/2017) is 02/28/2016. Looking at the options, option C is 02/28/2016, which matches this. Let me double-check the other options to make sure. Option A is 02/28/2015. If today is 2017, then one year ago would be 2016, so this is two years ago. Not correct. Option B is 03/31/2016. That seems like a random date. If we subtract one year from 02/28/2017, it's not March. Option D is 02/28/1992. That's way in the past, not one year ago. Option E is 03/27/2016. Again, no connection here. Option F is 03/06/2016. Also not relevant. Therefore, the correct answer should be option C. But let me re-verify the logic. Jane's birthday is on the last day of February each year. So in 2001, it was 02/28. In 2004 (a leap year), it would be 02/29. However, since Jane was born in 2001, her birthday is fixed on the last day of February regardless of leap years. So in 2016, which is a leap year, her 15th birthday would be on 02/29/2016. Wait, but wait—Jane turns 16 today. So if today is 02/28/2017, that's her 16th birthday. So in 2016, her 15th birthday would have been on 02/29/2016 because 2016 is a leap year. However, that doesn't affect the calculation of one year before today. The problem is not asking about her birthday one year ago, but the date one year ago from today. So today is 02/28/2017. One year prior is 02/28/2016.
